core_intuitions: The whole run is a chain of format-transforming stages (text -> tokens -> uint16 .bin -> model -> checkpoint -> samples); every stage's *internals* were built in another chapter, and THIS chapter is only the glue that makes the artifacts hand off cleanly.

The uint16 shard is a CONTRACT: prepare.py's writer dtype must exactly equal ShardedTokenLoader's memmap dtype, and the loader slices (ctx+1)-token windows that split into x = w[:-1] and y = w[1:] (the one-position target shift). A dtype mismatch reinterprets bytes as garbage; a missing shift makes loss stick at ln(vocab).

The SAME train.py scales across four hardware tiers; what changes is not the model/loss/schedule code but the launch flags and the sharding strategy -- 'use the least sharding that fits': DDP (replicate) while state fits one GPU, climb to FSDP FULL_SHARD (shard params/grads/optimizer), then HYBRID_SHARD when the inter-node link becomes the bottleneck.

The effective batch is assembled from four multiplied knobs (local_bsz x ctx x world_size x grad_accum), and that tokens/step number divides the Chinchilla token budget to set the total step count against which the LR schedule is computed.

A healthy run has a legible fingerprint: loss starts near ln(vocab) (a uniform guesser), drops fast, then grinds slowly; pre-clip grad-norm stays ~0.1-1.0; MFU sits ~40-55% on GPU. Deviations map to specific bugs (stuck at ln(vocab) = target-shift bug; NaN = fp16 overflow or Adam amplifying a bad batch).

new_figures.name: pretrain-pipeline-spine
new_figures.concept: The end-to-end run as a chain of nine stages, each labeled with the file that runs it, the artifact/format it emits, and the deep chapter that builds its internals.
new_figures.rationale: This IS the chapter's organizing spine -- its whole thesis is 'assemble the gears and point at the bolts that connect them.' The chapter currently only has a cramped ASCII box-diagram that wraps awkwardly and shows none of the format transitions or file names. A single clean infographic that makes the data-type handoffs visible (raw text -> integer IDs -> uint16 bytes on disk -> logits -> checkpoint -> sampled text) turns the 'lab manual' framing into one glanceable map and anchors every later stage back to a position in the flow.
new_figures.placement: The Whole Pipeline on One Page
new_figures.brief: A horizontal (wrapping to two rows) pipeline of nine numbered stages, read left-to-right then wrapping. Each stage is a rounded node containing: the stage number + short name, and BELOW it in mono the file/tool that runs it. Between consecutive nodes, put a small labeled 'artifact' tag on the connecting arrow showing WHAT is handed off and its concrete format -- this is the load-bearing content. Sequence: (0) Environment/repo layout [setup]; (1) Corpus -> Tokenizer [tiktoken gpt2] with tag 'raw text strings'; (2) Tokenize to shards [prepare.py] with tag 'integer IDs'; then a prominent tag on the arrow OUT of stage 2 reading 'uint16 .bin shards on disk' (make this handoff visually heavier -- a thicker arrow or a small disk/database glyph -- because the chapter calls it the single most important piece of glue); (3) Model [GPT class] with tag 'token IDs -> logits'; (4) Optimizer + LR schedule [AdamW + cosine-warmup] with tag 'gradients -> weights'; (5) Training loop [train.py under torchrun] with tag 'checkpoints (step_*.pt)'; (6) Monitoring [loss/gnorm/MFU]; (7) Evaluation [eval_ppl.py + lm_eval] with tag 'perplexity + benchmark'; (8) Inference/Sampling [sample.py] with tag 'generated text'. The training loop (5) should sit visually at the hub -- monitoring (6) reads its logs and checkpointing feeds resume; show a small feedback loop arrow from checkpoint back into the loop for resume, and a light dashed line from stages 6/7/8 all consuming a saved checkpoint. Along the bottom, a thin muted ribbon labeled 'each stage's internals are built in its own chapter -- this chapter is only the glue between artifacts', reinforcing the reuse-not-rederive theme. Use one accent to mark the two artifacts the chapter stresses as the true contracts (the uint16 shard between 2->3, and the checkpoint out of 5); keep everything else neutral. Theme-safe: no hardcoded brand colors, use currentColor + one theme-variable accent; ensure the mono file names and the small artifact tags stay legible at figure width. No invented numbers anywhere; vocab=50304 is the only concrete integer allowed (it appears in the text) and even that is optional.

new_figures.name: pretrain-four-scale-ladder
new_figures.concept: One train.py, four hardware tiers: what stays identical (code, shard format) versus what changes (launch flags + sharding strategy) as you climb from a laptop CPU to a multi-node cluster, framed as 'use the least sharding that fits.'
new_figures.rationale: This is the chapter's deepest conceptual payload and the answer to its Interview Corner question. The reader must internalize that scaling is NOT rewriting the model -- it is swapping a --parallel flag and a sharding strategy as memory and interconnect force your hand. A static ladder that puts the four tiers side by side, holds the code column constant, and shows model-state (params/grads/optimizer) transitioning from replicated (DDP) to sharded (FSDP FULL_SHARD) to hybrid, makes the 'least sharding that fits' principle and the two break-points (DDP OOMs -> FSDP; inter-node link saturates -> HYBRID_SHARD) click in one glance. It is distinct from 3.5's generic ZeRO-stage ladder: this one is indexed by THIS chapter's concrete hardware tiers and launch commands, not by ZeRO stage number.
new_figures.placement: Stage 5: The Training Loop — `torchrun` at Four Scales
new_figures.brief: Four columns, one per scale, left to right as a rising ladder: 'Laptop / CPU toy', 'Single GPU', '8-GPU node', 'Multi-node cluster'. Across the TOP, spanning all four columns, a persistent unchanged banner: 'same train.py -- same uint16 shard format -- same GPT / loss / cosine schedule' (this invariance is the whole point; make it visually fixed/shared). Within each column, stack three rows. Row A 'launch': the distinguishing flags in mono, kept minimal -- laptop: 'torchrun --nproc_per_node=1 --parallel ddp --backend gloo'; single GPU: 'torchrun --nproc_per_node=1 --parallel ddp'; 8-GPU: 'torchrun --nproc_per_node=8 --parallel fsdp (FULL_SHARD)'; multi-node: 'torchrun --nnodes=N ... --parallel fsdp (HYBRID_SHARD)'. Row B 'what each GPU holds': a small memory-box glyph illustrating model state (three sub-slabs: params / grads / optimizer-state) plus a separate 'activations' slab. For laptop + single GPU: the three model-state slabs are drawn FULL/replicated inside one device (label 'replicated: full copy per process'). For 8-GPU: the three model-state slabs are drawn as thin 1/N slivers (label 'sharded across 8 ranks -- FULL_SHARD') while the activations slab stays full (annotate 'activations are NOT sharded by data parallelism -- the usual OOM culprit'). For multi-node: show two node-boxes; model-state sharded WITHIN a node over fast NVLink, and only the lighter gradient sync crossing the slow inter-node link (label 'shard intra-node, replicate/sync across nodes -- HYBRID_SHARD'). Between the columns, place two break-point markers on the ascending arrow: between single-GPU and 8-GPU, a marker 'model state no longer fits one GPU -> shard it (DDP -> FSDP)'; between 8-GPU and multi-node, a marker 'inter-node all-gather becomes the bottleneck -> keep param-gathers on NVLink (FULL_SHARD -> HYBRID_SHARD)'. Add a short vertical caption down the side: 'least sharding that fits: climb only as far as memory / network forces you.' Use small illustrative device counts (1, 1, 8, and two nodes) only -- NO params/tokens/wall-clock numbers, NO MFU numbers (those live in the recipe table). Theme-safe accents only; the sliver-vs-full-slab contrast must read clearly in both light and dark. Keep the four columns inside the viewBox with the mono flag strings wrapping or abbreviated rather than overflowing.

new_figures.name: pretrain-shard-contract-window
new_figures.concept: The uint16 shard as a two-sided contract: prepare.py writes tokens as uint16 bytes, ShardedTokenLoader memmaps with the identical dtype, then slices a (ctx+1)-token window into x = w[:-1] and y = w[1:] -- the one-position target shift.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter names this 'the single most important piece of glue' and returns to it twice: the writer/loader dtype must match exactly (mismatch = silently reinterpreted garbage) and the (ctx+1) window's one-position shift is exactly the off-by-one whose failure makes loss stick at ln(vocab) in Stage 7. A compact static figure that shows the byte stream, the matching-dtype handshake, and the window splitting into aligned input/target rows makes both the contract and the most common 'model won't learn' bug intuitive in one picture -- something the prose can only assert.
new_figures.brief: Three stacked bands connected top-to-bottom. TOP band 'writer -- prepare.py': a short row of token cells [The][ transformer][ processes][ ...][<EOT>] above a matching row of small integer IDs, feeding into a contiguous byte-ribbon labeled 'np.array(..., dtype=uint16).tofile()  ->  train_000.bin'. Draw each token as occupying exactly 2 bytes in the ribbon and annotate '2 bytes/token because vocab 50304 < 65536'. MIDDLE handshake strip: a two-headed lock/handshake glyph spanning writer and reader with the caption 'the contract: writer dtype == loader dtype'. On one side 'prepare.py writes uint16', on the other 'ShardedTokenLoader: np.memmap(dtype=uint16)'. Include a small muted warning callout: 'mismatch (e.g. read as int32) -> every ID silently wraps -> training corrupts'. BOTTOM band 'reader -- one (ctx+1) window': from the same byte-ribbon, bracket out a window of ctx+1 consecutive tokens, then split it into TWO horizontally-aligned rows offset by exactly one cell: top row x = w[:-1] labeled 'input', bottom row y = w[1:] labeled 'target (next token)'. Draw thin vertical connectors between each x cell and the y cell one position to its right, so the 'predict the next token' shift is visually literal. Add a small annotation: 'drop the shift and loss sticks at ln(vocab) -- the classic Stage 7 bug'. Keep integers tiny and illustrative (a handful of made-up small IDs is fine; do not invent any benchmark or size numbers beyond the vocab=50304 that appears in the text and 65536 as the uint16 ceiling). Theme-safe: token cells and byte cells outlined with currentColor, one accent to highlight the aligned shift and the matching-dtype lock. Everything must fit the viewBox with no overlapping labels; the shift connectors must stay legible.

summary: This capstone 'glue' chapter has zero figures despite being concept-dense; it is figure-poor and intuition-rich, so I propose three foundational static infographics and no animations (nothing here is a multi-step temporal process that a static frame cannot carry). (1) pretrain-pipeline-spine upgrades the chapter's cramped ASCII box-diagram into a real nine-stage map that makes the format handoffs (text -> IDs -> uint16 .bin -> logits -> checkpoint -> samples) and the reuse-not-rederive framing glanceable -- the chapter's organizing spine. (2) pretrain-four-scale-ladder visualizes the chapter's deepest payload and Interview-Corner answer: one train.py across four hardware tiers, holding the code constant while the launch flags and sharding strategy climb DDP -> FSDP FULL_SHARD -> HYBRID_SHARD under the 'least sharding that fits' principle, with the two break-points marked; it is deliberately indexed by THIS chapter's hardware tiers, not a duplicate of 3.5's generic ZeRO-stage ladder. (3) pretrain-shard-contract-window makes the 'single most important piece of glue' concrete -- the writer/loader dtype handshake and the (ctx+1) window's one-position x/y shift, which doubles as the picture of the Stage-7 'loss stuck at ln(vocab)' target-shift bug. All three are theme-safe, invent no benchmark numbers, and are fully legible as static frames.
